data.title: Conan O'Brien: Correspondents' dinner crowd 'think they're the coolest people in the world'
data.description:
Former White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner entertainer Conan O’Brien says the audience at the black-tie event is far from a comedian’s typical crowd, and they “think they’re the coolest people in the world.”

“That is not a normal crowd,” O’Brien said of Correspondents’ dinner revelers, while he appeared on a new episode of Michelle Obama and Craig Robinson’s “IMO” podcast, released Wednesday.

“They’re thinking a lot about their own world. And you need to be funny, but you can’t act like you know too much, because then they’ll resent you for that, or think you don’t belong,” the former late-night TV host explained.

O’Brien served as the headliner at the 2013 WHCA dinner.

The comic opened up about the process of attempting to entertain the journalists, lawmakers and celebrity guests that typically attend the Washington gala.

“Everything is this incredible long period of preparation to gauge what’s going to work in that room that doesn’t go too far this way, doesn’t go too far that way,” O’Brien, 62, said.

President Trump, who bucked tradition and boycotted the dinner throughout his first term, announced this week that he planned on attending the April 25 gala.

“Will be fun!” Trump wrote in a social media post.

In his conversation with the former first lady, O’Brien recounted a conversation he had with former President Obama about the event, which has earned the nickname “Nerd Prom” over the years. 

“I’ve talked to your husband about this,” he told Obama.

“They make you follow the leader of the free world,” O’Brien said, referring to commanders in chief who traditionally deliver roast-like remarks at the dinner before the night’s entertainer takes the stage.

“He goes first,” O’Brien said, “And kills it.”

“Then he would finish and like drop the mic, crowd going crazy,” O’Brien continued of following Obama.

“And a voice would go, ‘Ladies and gentleman, Conan O’Brien,'” the comedian continued, muffling his name while imitating the announcer.

“You see people like, ‘What? Who would go now?’ So you got to wade through that a little bit. But it’s all calculating for what’s the assignment,” O’Brien, who’s poised to return as host of the Academy Awards this month, said.

Likening the Correspondents’ dinner to the Oscars, O’Brien said, “Now I’m going to speak to a bunch of A-list people who don’t really like to give it up for anybody. But it’s all just a calculation. You got to calculate and then have fun.”

O’Brien, who hosted his TV show, “Conan,” from 2010 to 2021, said he’s friends with today’s field of late-night comics. 

Asked by Obama if he felt that “comedy is more fraught these days,” O’Brien replied, “I think they’re all very talented. They’re really good people and they’re in a tough situation because your job is to go out and talk about the news every day.”

“I was never strictly a comedian that lives off the news. I usually tried to find things that were silly and funny outside of the news, but we also had to do the news,” he said.

“Things are so divisive right now,” O’Brien said of comedy in the Trump era, without mentioning the president by name.

“I think it’s got to be just a really tough task and I do have empathy for people who are trying to figure out what’s funny in this,” he said, “because I know just as a citizen, I get up in the morning and I look at what happened in the news.”

“I like to be funny, but my first instinct when I read the news is nothing here gives me joy or is making me laugh that hard,” the “Conan O’Brien Needs a Friend” podcast host told Obama.

“But if you’re doing one of those shows, how do you not talk about it?” O’Brien said.

“How do you not wear your heart on your sleeve? Maybe get mad. So it’s difficult.”

data.title: Kelly: Iran operation 'not going well' for Trump administration
data.description:
Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) sharply criticized the Trump administration’s launch of Operation Epic Fury against Iran last weekend. 

“They don’t seem to know what they’re doing or why they did this,” Kelly told MS NOW on Wednesday morning. 

“I think this is a perfect example of what happens when you have a leader of a country who doesn’t take input, doesn’t take advice, puts a lot of yes people around him and makes these decisions without the right input and without the right data,” the senator continued. “So, I think this is not going well for them.”

Kelly sits on the Senate Armed Services Committee and has pushed back on the White House following the Saturday launch of joint strikes from Israel and the U.S. on Iran, which killed Iranian supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. 

Six American servicemembers have been killed so far during the conflict. 

Trump officials have been critical of the media’s coverage of the Iranian counterstrikes, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth accusing outlets of reporting on the deaths of these service members to “make the president look bad” during a press briefing at the Pentagon on Wednesday. 

“This is what the fake news misses. We’ve taken control of Iran’s airspace and waterways without boots on the ground. We control their fate. But when a few drones get through or tragic things happen, it’s front-page news,” Hegseth said at the Pentagon. “I get it, the press only wants to make the president look bad but try for once to report the reality.” 

Kelly slammed the defense secretary’s words in a post on the social platform X on Wednesday. 

“There is nothing more sacred than the lives of our servicemembers and they deserve a president and a secretary of Defense who respect their service and sacrifice,” Kelly said.

data.title: Mark Zuckerberg buys $170M home on 'billionaire bunker' island in Miami
data.description:
(NewsNation) – Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and his wife, Priscilla Chan, have purchased their new home on Miami’s “billionaire bunker island” for $170 million.

Their recent purchase on Indian Creek Island sets a record for Miami-Dade County, and is one of the U.S.’ most expensive home purchases to date, according to The Wall Street Journal. Billionaire Ken Griffin holds the record for his nearly $238 million purchase of a New York apartment in 2019.

It will be some time before they move in, however, as construction is still ongoing at the home. Some of the nine-bedroom, 11 1/2-bathroom home’s newest features are set to include a gym, hair salon and massage room, along with a 1,500-gallon aquarium and a library with a secret passageway.

Details of an actual bunker on Zuckerberg’s new property are unclear. But in recent years, ultra-wealthy buyers have been purchasing homes with bunkers or bomb shelters on-site, NewsNation previously reported. A bunker manufacturer, Atlas Survival Shelters, makes and installs bunkers on the properties of people who request them, totaling an average cost of $25,000

“The world’s elites are getting bunkers, and most of the billionaires, if they don’t have a bunker, they are probably getting one right now,” Rob Hubbard of Atlas Survival Shelters told NewsNation.

“It’s not that they are worried about an immediate scare or something imminent, they just know there are people out there who have feelings different than you and I might have about the future, and they want to be prepared for that,” Hubbard continued.

When Zuckerberg purchased a home in Hawaii in early 2025, he denied reports of having a doomsday bunker on the property, instead describing it as “more of a basement.”

data.title: Iranian leader of Trump assassination plot killed, Hegseth says
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Wednesday said the Iranian leader who planned to assassinate President Trump was killed.

“Yesterday, the leader of the unit who attempted to assassinate President Trump has been hunted down and killed. Iran tried to kill President Trump, and President Trump got the last laugh,” Hegseth said during a press briefing.

In 2024, the Justice Department unsealed a criminal complaint revealing that Farhad Shakeri, 51, had been charged with murder-for-hire.

He allegedly told FBI agents in September 2024 that an Iranian official tasked him with assassinating Trump. Further details about Shakeri were not shared at the time.

The killing comes as the U.S. and Israel plans further attacks on Iran, Hegseth said. 

“Now, this is not a mission accomplished situation. This is simply a reality check. The combination of U.S. and Israeli intelligence and combat power will control Iran, and will control it soon,” he told reporters.

“Sure, Iran will still be able to shoot some missiles and still be able to launch one-way attack drones at civilian targets, and their proxies will attempt to attack our embassies, bases, and soft targets,” he added.

Hegseth continued, “They are terrorists, after all, and they need to target civilians because they can’t fight toe-to-toe. But we will find them, and we will kill them.”

So far, Iran has retaliated against two U.S. embassies abroad and killed six Americans sheltering at a civilian port in Kuwait.

data.title: Johnson: 'Everybody in America better watch' how lawmakers vote on DHS funding
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) told reporters on Wednesday that “everybody in America” should watch how their representatives vote on the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding bill expected to come to the floor on Thursday.

“You better watch that board. And everybody in America better watch that board. Anybody who votes to block funding for the homeland, it is shameful,” Johnson said. “I don’t know how to describe it. It speaks to a long record of Democrats’ deliberate efforts to undermine America’s safety and the essential operations of DHS.”

Democrats have demanded that the White House significantly reform its immigration enforcement tactics after the killings of two U.S. citizens by federal agents in Minnesota, and have refused to support any DHS bill that comes to the floor without those reforms. But the White House and Democrats have been deadlocked over a deal to reopen DHS, which shut down early Feb. 14 after a two-week stopgap measure to keep the department funded at existing levels expired.

Republicans have been ramping up pressure on Democrats to support the DHS bill in the wake of the U.S. attack on Iran. The bill is largely identical to the one that had passed the lower chamber in January, before the death of 37-year-old Minneapolis nurse Alex Pretti.

Democratic leadership have been opposed to the bill, however. 

“Donald Trump launches an unauthorized war in the Middle East. He characterizes it as endless. He decides that he wants to spend billions of dollars to bomb Iran, rather than spend taxpayer dollars to lower the grocery bills that are crushing the American people,” House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said during a press conference on Tuesday.

“And then wants to use his unauthorized war as an excuse to continue spending taxpayer dollars to brutalize or kill American citizens by continuing to unleash ICE without restrictions on the American people. The whole thing is insane. Make it make sense, because it does not,” Jeffries added. 

A whip notice shared with Politico had urged House Democrats to vote against the bill, arguing it contains “no new language to end the chaos caused by ICE in communities across the country.” 

Johnson argued during the press conference that the military action in Iran “makes it all more urgent and crucial to have a fully funded, fully staffed DHS across all its departments.”

“We have Democrats running around here playing political games in Congress. It’s infuriating. They shut down the very agency that is responsible for securing the homeland. The Democrats have shut down the agency responsible for keeping Americans safe. Unbelievable,” Johnson said.

The bill is expected to pass the House, but will likely be dead on arrival in the Senate, where it will need some Democratic support to overcome a filibuster.

data.title: Don't let Congress abuse policy to give away public lands
data.description:
When lawmakers resort to a loose interpretation of a rarely used law to open public lands to mining, we need to call it what it is: desperate.  

The mess began in fall of last year, when Congress quietly began using the 1996 Congressional Review Act to overturn longstanding land-use plans that govern vast areas of public lands in Wyoming, Montana, North Dakota and Alaska, opening them to extractive industries.  

Emboldened by those decisions, Rep. Pete Stauber (R-Minn.) and the Utah congressional delegation, led by Rep. Celeste Maloy (R-Utah), are now going after two beloved landscapes.

On Jan. 12, Stauber invoked the Congressional Review Act to overturn a 20-year mining ban in the headwaters of the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness. On Jan. 26, the Government Accountability Office ruled in Maloy’s favor and found Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument’s management plan eligible to be overturned using the same process. 

This is a cynical departure from the Congressional Review Act’s origin as part of a package of reforms meant to help small businesses navigate federal regulations. And it is quickly becoming a reliable tool for extractive-industry-aligned lawmakers. 

From 1996 to 2017, the Congressional Review Act was used only once — on an Occupational Safety and Health Administration regulation. After President Trump first took office in 2017, however, Congress used it to overturn 16 Obama administration regulations. When Trump returned to the White House last year, 22 more rules were nullified using the same process. 

The Trump administration and lawmakers working under its influence are wielding the act not as a regulatory scalpel but as a policy guillotine, to drop on anything that gets in the way of their agenda. Including public land protections.

Land management policies are the result of extensive legal and scientific review and considerable public input. They are a model of responsible policymaking. Tourism and extractive industries alike rely on these policies and the predictability they engender to operate with confidence. To overturn them with no respect for that which goes into making them is nothing short of undemocratic. 

Seventy percent of Minnesotans, for example, support protecting the Boundary Waters permanently, and 56 percent of people in Stauber’s district oppose mining there. Even President Teddy Roosevelt’s great-grandson weighed in on the matter, urging Republican senators to vote it down. 

Stauber’s release celebrating the House passing the resolution is rife with Trumpian buzzwords, claiming that a mine would protect Minnesota jobs and bolster national security. In reality, studies find the mine threatens up to 22,000 jobs connected to tourism and the $1.6 billion that tourism generates. Additionally, an independent study from 2020 found that mining would likely cause a net-negative effect on the regional economy.

As for national security, there is a strong chance that Antofagasta, the Chilean company that would own the operation, could ship the minerals it extracts to China to refine and sell on the global market. That would at the least seem to be contrary to U.S. national interests.

In Utah, Grand Staircase-Escalante’s national monument designation would remain in place, but its management plan would be overturned, putting at risk everything that designation is meant to protect.

Maloy’s action flies in the face of the 89 percent of Utah voters who say the region’s tribes should have a strong role in managing ancestral lands, and the majority of Utahns who agree with Grand Staircase-Escalante’s current management plan.

The local economy benefited there, too. Between 2001 and 2022, jobs in the area increased by 51 percent, and income per capita by 41 percent. Outdoor recreation contributed more than $9.7 billion to Utah’s economy in 2023. One would be justified in questioning how any of this is “America first” while study after study shows that protecting public lands benefits local economies in a sustainable way.

From a business point of view, it is difficult to consider Stauber and Maloy’s actions without seeing the potential harm to locals — the outfitters, grocers, guides, campgrounds and hotels. Entire industries may get caught in the crossfire, including the $1.2 trillion outdoor recreation industry, which relies on access to places like the Boundary Waters and Grand Staircase-Escalante.  

The outdoor industry — and, ironically, the extractive industry — depend on clear, predictable public lands management. Weaponization of the the Congressional Review Act may well lead to a more conservation-minded Congress using it later to invalidate land management plans that allow for drilling, mining or logging.

It is not difficult to imagine an endless vortex of legal challenges and congressional review votes that freeze businesses connected to public lands.  

The good news is we have experience pushing back against congressional attempts to liquidate public lands. We have proven that mass societal pressure is effective.

Affected businesses must organize and tell Congress that its actions threaten their bottom lines. Activists and recreationists must continue filling inboxes, flooding the streets and making calls demanding our representatives actually represent us. When people and businesses emphatically remind Congress our public lands are not for sale, lawmakers listen.  

It may be the only precedent we can count on. 

Ryan Gellert is CEO of Patagonia, Inc.

data.title: Ohio Republican: Americans 'right to be skeptical' about Iran operation
data.description:
Rep. Warren Davidson (R-Ohio) on Tuesday said Americans have every “right to be skeptical” about the U.S.-Israeli military operation in Iran.

Davidson responded to context given by CNN anchor Boris Sanchez about the “somewhat dubious, only at least on the surface” rationale that the administration provided after launching airstrikes on Saturday.

Sanchez named off the Pentagon’s assessment that Iran would not strike unless provoked, then the contradicting claims that Iran was close to developing a nuclear weapon.

“Well, look, I think the public’s right to be skeptical, and, frankly, that’s part of why President Trump’s, you know, ‘no new wars’ kind of campaign mindset resonated with the public,” Davidson replied. “You look at war-weary people who’ve seen just endless wars really in this century so far, post-9/11, the U.S. has kind of been in continual war.”

Davidson made similar comments in a separate CNN interview later Tuesday, where he questioned the Trump administration’s justification for the attack. He noted that he has asked for a classified briefing with administration officials before the strikes occurred to “define the mission in Iran.”

“And we‘re seeing that today with multiple definitions,” he told correspondent Kaitlan Collins.

Davidson told Sanchez that he anticipates a House vote on a resolution under the War Powers Act that seeks to keep Trump in check on Iran. The Ohio Republican is so far one of just two GOP House lawmakers backing the resolution, the other being co-sponsor and outspoken Trump critic Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.).

“Look, the idea that Congress doesn’t vote isn’t unprecedented,” he said about Congress approving declarations of war, as mandated by the Constitution. “Congress never voted on the Korean War, but it was a war, even though they tried to say it was something other than that while it was being waged.”

The House last formally declared war against Bulgaria, Hungary and Romania in 1942 as the U.S. entered World War II, according to the Senate’s website. The month before, the U.S. declared war on the Axis powers following Japan’s attack on Pearl Harbor on Dec. 7, 1941.

The Korean War “was a de facto war and I think that’s the reality — is we’re in a de facto state of war with Iran right now,” Davidson added.

Rep. Tim Burchett (R-Tenn.) similarly advised voters to “stay concerned” in case operations in Iran become “another forever war.”

Voters have said they are skeptical about the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran. A CNN poll on Monday found that 56 percent of respondents said they believed “a long-term military conflict between the US and Iran” is “very likely” or “somewhat likely.”

Pollsters also found that 30 percent believe a long-term conflict is “somewhat unlikely” or “very unlikely.”

data.title: Raskin presses Noem on whether she'd still label Pretti, Good as domestic terrorists
data.description:
Rep. Jamie Raskin (Md.), the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, asked Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem whether knowing what she knows today, she would still label Renee Good and Alex Pretti as domestic terrorists.

The ranking member framed his question as “a chance before the entire country to correct your false and defamatory claim.” 

Noem repeated her sympathies to their families and said “their lives will never be the same,” but did not retract the claims, saying she was trying to “offer as much information as we can from officers and agents on the ground in a chaotic scene.”

“There’s ongoing investigations that are being led,” Noem said, before being cut off by Raskin.

“You didn’t wait for the evidence. You proclaimed that they were domestic terrorists at the time. Why did you do that?” he asked.

Noem continued to reference the FBI-led investigation into the deaths, which has barred coordination with local authorities.

“So you still don’t know? You think that’s an open question?” Raskin said.

“You stated the conclusion two hours after they were killed that they were domestic terrorists.”

Noem accused Raskin of also criticizing the officers involved before he had all the information.

Raskin’s line of questioning took a different track than those in the Senate Judiciary Committee Tuesday, who had encouraged her to apologize for the remarks.

data.title: Comer on sending US troops into Iran: 'Sometimes that's unavoidable'
data.description:
House Oversight Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) on Wednesday said sometimes deploying troops overseas is “unavoidable” while addressing the current situation in Iran following U.S. strikes on the nation over the weekend.

“The president has yet to rule that out. I certainly hope not,” Comer told NewsNation’s “Morning in America,” referring to the potential of sending troops to Iran.

“If there are boots on the ground, I hope they’re not on the ground any longer than the boots on the ground were in Venezuela,” he continued. “But I think that that’s something the president knows that members of Congress certainly hope doesn’t happen.”

Comer continued, “But sometimes that’s unavoidable in a situation like this.”

Since the initial strikes on Tehran, at least six Americans have been killed and two U.S. embassies abroad have been struck by drones. 

The Army has publicly identified four of the six victims. Sgt. 1st Class Nicole Amor died alongside Capt. Cody Khork of Lakeland, Fla., Sgt. 1st Class Noah Tietjens of Bellevue, Neb., and Sgt. Declan Coady of Des Moines, Iowa. 

The other two have not been named.

On Wednesday, Comer shared his condolences for the individuals who lost their lives. 

“Some had been from Fort Knox in Kentucky, my home state,” he told NewsNation’s Markie Martin. “So, you know, we’re … devastated. And obviously our thoughts and prayers are with the families of anyone that’s been lost or wounded thus far.”

President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth have signaled that they expect more American casualties. 

Hegseth also said the Pentagon hasn’t ruled out putting troops on the ground in Iran. 

“You don’t have to roll 200,000 people in there and stay for 20 years. We’ve proven that you can achieve objectives that advance American interests without being foolish,” the Defense chief said during a Monday press briefing. “Now, will we be bold about it?”

He added, “Are we going to be decisive about it? Put months and months of planning into what kind of effects we want to achieve? Absolutely.”

data.title: Newsom accepts 'Cup O Peace' award from Kimmel: 'Eat your heart out, Donald'
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) has a message for President Trump after being presented with a satirical peace award on Jimmy Kimmel’s late-night TV show: “Eat your heart out, Donald.”

“I know you have an ongoing back-and-forth with the president, as do I,” the ABC “Jimmy Kimmel Live!” host told Newsom on his Tuesday show.

“We have a surprise tonight that I think is going to make him very jealous,” added Kimmel, one of Trump’s most vocal late-night critics. 

A Newsom impersonator then rolled out on a skateboard to greet the “Young Man in a Hurry” author. 

“I’m so excited to be here for a Newsom twosome,” the faux governor exclaimed. 

“Gov. Gav, for your tireless work in uniting the people of Cali — from the meth dealers of Riverside to the shapely Armenians of the Glendale Galleria, from chakra doulas and yoga thems, to farmers market furries and rollerbladers with gluten sensitivities — I, governor Gavin D. Newsom, in association with the wounded Waymo project and the three remaining original members of Incubus, present to you, Gov. Gavin C. Newsom, with the 2026 FIFA Skydance Paramount Netflix Warner Bros. Big Ass Lipton Cup O’ Peace,” the look-a-like told the real-life Newsom.

“Unbelievable,” the Golden State’s governor responded. 

“Look, this is long overdue. And I assume it comes with a third term?” Newsom, a potential 2028 White House hopeful, cracked. 

“I just want to thank me for this unbelievable award,” he added.

In a Wednesday post on X, Newsom thanked Kimmel in an all-caps message mocking Trump.

“Many people are saying this is the most prestigious award for peace any one has ever received in the history of mankind and it will replace the Nobel Peace Prize – which is outdated, and simply not large enough!” Newsom wrote.

The Kimmel bit came after Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado presented her Nobel Peace Prize to Trump during a January meeting at the White House. The president has long made the push for being a recipient of the annual honor for his role in ending global conflicts. Last year, Trump said it would be a “big insult” to the United States if he wasn’t awarded the prize.

In December, FIFA President Gianni Infantino awarded Trump the inaugural FIFA Peace Prize, which was created by the global soccer governing body ahead of the World Cup.
